description: The provided image shows a generic "Check for updates" button icon, indicating the target scientific figure from the low-density δ-ferrite steel research fails to load, so no valid material science related visual content including micrographs, spectra, performance curves or schematic diagrams can be directly identified from the current image. The associated paper focuses on Fe-based low-density δ-ferrite steel with 4.5 wt% Al, investigating effects of rolling temperatures at 650℃,750℃,850℃ and 950℃ on microstructure evolution, carbide precipitation behavior and mechanical properties. Samples rolled below 750℃ form lamellar structures with dispersed ultra-fine spherical carbides, showing excellent impact toughness up to 207.5 J, while samples rolled above 850℃ form δ-ferrite/martensite dual-phase structures with poor impact toughness lower than 7 J.

This figure contains three subgraphs introducing experimental design for low-density δ-ferrite steel research. Subgraph (a) is a schematic temperature-time plot of the thermomechanical processing route: the steel ingot is homogenized at 1200℃ for 4 h, hot forged, air cooled, then held at 650℃, 750℃, 850℃, 950℃ respectively for 2 h, followed by hot rolling with 60% reduction and air cooling to obtain four sample groups (R650, R750, R850, R950), with the Ac1 critical temperature marked. Subgraph (b) displays the geometry of the tensile specimen, sampled along rolling direction (RD), with a 25 mm gauge length and 5 mm gauge diameter. Subgraph (c) shows the Charpy V-notch impact specimen with dimensions of 10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm, with the notch machined on the rolled surface. This figure clarifies processing parameters and mechanical test specimen specifications to support subsequent microstructure-property correlation analysis.

This group of optical microscopy (OM) images presents the microstructure evolution of low-density δ-ferrite steel processed at four different rolling temperatures: subfigure (a) corresponds to sample R650 rolled at 650℃, (b) to R750 rolled at 750℃, (c) to R850 rolled at 850℃, (d) to R950 rolled at 950℃, with a uniform 50μm scale bar for all subfigures, and rolling direction (RD) and normal direction (ND) marked in (a). Low-temperature rolled samples (R650, R750) exhibit alternating lamellar structure: light-colored bands are δ-ferrite, dark bands are carbide/ferrite composite lamellae, the proportion of carbide/ferrite lamellae decreases from 51.5% to 43.3% as rolling temperature increases from 650 to 750℃, matching the thermodynamically predicted carbide stable range below 750℃. High-temperature rolled samples (R850, R950) show fully dissolved carbides, forming duplex lamellar structure of light δ-ferrite and dark honeycomb martensite, with small equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite distributed in martensite lamellae. This figure verifies that rolling temperature dominates phase constitution and lamellar morphology of the tested steel, providing morphological evidence for the relationship between rolling process, microstructure and mechanical performance.

This figure consists of paired low-magnification SEM and high-magnification TEM micrographs, plus carbide size distribution histograms, to reveal the effect of hot rolling temperature on the microstructure of low-density δ-ferrite steel. Subplots (a-c) depict the as-hot-forged sample before rolling, composed of coarse δ-ferrite and martensite/austenite constituents without obvious lamellar structure. (d-f) show sample R650 (rolled at 650 °C), featuring lamellar structure with ultra-fine spherical carbides (average diameter 158.3±76.9 nm, distribution density 1.7±0.2 μm⁻²) dispersed in the ferrite matrix of composite lamellae. (g-i) correspond to sample R750 (rolled at 750 °C), where carbides are coarsened to 247.3±86.8 nm with reduced density of 0.6±0.1 μm⁻² following Ostwald ripening. (j-o) for samples R850 (850 °C) and R950 (950 °C) show fully dissolved carbides, with microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing deformation-induced equiaxed ferrite. The figure confirms that low-temperature rolling enables dispersed ultra-fine spheroidized carbides, which is critical for achieving superior impact toughness of the tested steel.

This figure presents Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of low-density δ-ferrite steel samples rolled at four different temperatures: R650 (650℃), R750 (750℃), R850 (850℃), R950 (950℃). It contains four sets of subgraphs: (a-d) Grain Boundary (GB) combined with Grain Orientation Spread (GOS) maps, where gray lines represent 2-15° low-angle grain boundaries, black lines represent >15° high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs), and green regions indicate areas with GOS ≤2°; (e-h) Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps, reflecting local misorientation and dislocation density; (i-l) Inverse Pole Figure (IPF) maps, showing grain orientation distribution; (m-p) IPF maps overlaid with nanoindentation marks, used to measure nanohardness of different phases. Key trends observed: low-temperature rolled samples (R650, R750) show higher KAM values (0.48-0.55°) corresponding to higher dislocation density, HAGB proportions of 43.5-49.6%, and GOS ≤2° proportions of 6.9-12.3%; high-temperature rolled samples (R850, R950) have lower KAM values (0.3-0.4°), increased HAGB proportions of 60.1-75.7%, higher GOS ≤2° proportions of 19.4-25.8%, and grain orientation shifted from <001> to <101> dominant. Nanoindentation results show the hardness difference between phases is only 0.72 GPa for low-temperature rolled samples, while it reaches 3.86 GPa for high-temperature rolled samples, which easily causes stress concentration at phase boundaries. This figure supports the conclusion that rolling temperature regulates the microstructure and mechanical properties of the tested steel: low-temperature rolling with dispersed ultra-fine spheroidized carbides improves strength and toughness via Orowan dislocation pinning, while large hardness mismatch in high-temperature rolled samples degrades impact toughness.

This figure consists of 6 subplots illustrating the mechanical properties of low-density δ-ferrite steel samples rolled at four different temperatures: R650 (650℃), R750 (750℃), R850 (850℃), R950 (950℃). Subplot (a) shows room-temperature impact energy decreases sharply with rising rolling temperature, from ~207.5 J for R650 to less than 6 J for R850/R950. Subplots (b,c) are impact load-displacement curves: low-temperature rolled R650/R750 exhibit distinct toughness propagation stages with measurable crack initiation energy (Ei) and propagation energy (Ep), while high-temperature rolled R850/R950 show steep single peaks with negligible Ei/Ep, indicating immediate unstable crack propagation after initiation. Subplot (d) is engineering stress-strain curves, showing ultimate tensile strength increases from ~869 MPa (R650) to ~1255 MPa (R950) as elongation drops from ~19% to ~3.5%. Subplots (e,f) are work-hardening rate curves: R650/R750 have a characteristic hump shape reflecting good deformation coordination, while R850/R950 show monotonic decrease, indicating poor strain hardening capacity. The figure demonstrates that low rolling temperature (≤750℃) enables excellent strength-toughness-plasticity synergy via dispersed ultra-fine spheroidized carbides, while higher rolling temperature causes severe toughness and plasticity loss.

This figure contains two subplots of thermodynamic calculation results for the studied low-density δ-ferrite steel. Subplot (a) is the iron-carbon equilibrium phase diagram, with x-axis representing C content (0.0 to 1.5 wt.%) and y-axis representing temperature (600 to 1450 °C), labeling phase regions including single BCC, single FCC, BCC+FCC, BCC+FCC+θ, and BCC+Carbides. Subplot (b) shows the volume fraction of Carbides, BCC, and FCC phases as a function of temperature from 500 °C to 1200 °C: carbides only exist stably below ~720 °C at low fraction, BCC fraction decreases while FCC fraction increases as temperature rises above 720 °C, with their fractions crossing at ~900 °C. This figure verifies that carbides are thermodynamically stable below 750 °C, which is consistent with experimental microstructural observations of rolled samples, and provides theoretical basis for selecting rolling temperature to regulate carbide precipitation.

This figure displays transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping results of R650 low-density δ-ferrite steel (hot rolled at 650 °C). Subfigure (a) shows the overall morphology of precipitated carbides with a 125 nm scale bar. Subfigures (b), (c), and (d) present the morphologies of M₃C, M₂₃C₆, and M₇C₃ carbides respectively, with corresponding SAED patterns for phase identification, equipped with scale bars of 125 nm (b) and 50 nm (c, d). The subsequent EDS maps exhibit the distribution of Al, C, Mn, and Cr, confirming that carbide regions are enriched in C, Mn and Cr while depleted in Al, matching the SAED phase identification results. These findings verify that multiple spheroidized carbide types precipitate outside δ-ferrite lamellae in former martensite regions after martensite fragmentation, supporting the formation mechanism of ferrite/carbide composite lamellae that enables excellent impact toughness of low-temperature rolled δ-ferrite steel.

This figure presents microstructures of deformation zones adjacent to fracture surfaces for R650 low-density δ-ferrite steel (rolled at 650 °C, 60% thickness reduction, air-cooled). Subgraphs (a, b) are optical microscopy (OM) images of impact fracture deformation zones, (c) is OM of tensile fracture deformation zone, all with 10 μm scale bars. Subgraphs (d-g) are scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of impact fracture deformation zones (10 μm scale bars for d-f, 5 μm for g), and (h, i) are SEM of tensile fracture deformation zones (5 μm scale bars). The micrographs reveal alternating lamellar structures of soft δ-ferrite and hard carbide/ferrite composite lamellae, with dispersed ultra-fine spherical carbides (<250 nm diameter) in the composite lamellae. Characteristic fracture features are observed: blunted crack tips when penetrating composite lamellae (d), main cracks with secondary horizontal cracks along lamellae (e, g), microvoids/microcracks nucleated at carbide-ferrite interfaces (f), and cracks propagating parallel to lamellar orientation in tensile test samples (h, i). These observations validate that the lamellar structure and dispersed spherical carbides improve toughness by blunting crack tips, dispersing stress concentration, inducing microvoid nucleation to release stress, and extending crack propagation paths, supporting the superior impact toughness and strength-plasticity performance of the R650 sample.

This figure presents impact fracture deformed zone characterization and corresponding fracture mechanism schematics for low-density δ-ferrite steels rolled at different temperatures. Subfigures (a,c) are low-magnification TEM (1 μm scale) of the deformed zone for R650 sample, (b,d) are HRTEM (5 nm scale) with inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) revealing atomic shear and dislocations at interfaces, verifying defect interactions with precipitates. Subfigures (e-j) are fracture schematics: (e,h) for R650, showing dispersed ultra-fine spheroidized carbides in ferrite/carbide lamellae that promote crack blunting, deflection and microvoid nucleation, enabling high impact toughness of 207.5 J; (f,i) for R750, showing coarsened, lower-density carbides that reduce energy dissipation, leading to ~60% lower impact energy than R650; (g,j) for R850/R950, showing fully dissolved carbides, δ-ferrite/honeycomb martensite dual-phase structure with deformation-induced ferrite (DIF) grains that act as crack propagation shortcuts, resulting in brittle fracture with impact energy <10 J. The figure confirms that fine dispersed spheroidized carbides are critical to achieve superior impact toughness of the studied low-density steel.
